context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Civil Society: NGOs and Social Movements

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
There is no doubt about the mission of NGOs for promoting development. How good are NGOs as development agents? In what circumstances are they more effective as agents of development? What are their limitations? What is the role of other civil society actors in development such as social movements? What is the boundary of civil society as an actor in particular development contexts?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection about NGOs and social movements in development, and I’m kind of unsure how to frame the argument. Could you help me figure out a clear position on when NGOs are effective, what their limits are, and how social movements fit in?
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point! Before we talk structure, let's figure out what you actually think. From the readings or your own knowledge — when you picture an NGO working in a developing country, what comes to mind as their biggest strength as a development agent?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the start of the assignment and asking for help framing their argument. Rather than scaffolding the structure for them, I should use the Socratic method to get them thinking about their own position first. I'll ask them what they already think about NGO effectiveness to activate prior knowledge and set the direction of discovery.

exchanges.student: I think their biggest strength is that they can be closer to local communities than the state sometimes, so they may understand needs faster and respond more flexibly. But I’m not fully sure if that’s always true, because some NGOs also seem pretty donor-driven.
exchanges.tutor: You've actually put your finger on a real tension in the literature. So let's pull on that thread — if an NGO is primarily accountable to its donors rather than the communities it serves, what does that do to the 'closeness to communities' strength you just described?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified two important tensions on their own: local responsiveness vs. donor-drivenness. This is genuinely good thinking and maps well onto the assignment's core questions about effectiveness and limitations. I should affirm the insight and push them to develop the tension further — specifically, what does donor-drivenness actually do to an NGO's effectiveness? This moves toward the 'limitations' part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: Yeah, then that strength gets weaker or maybe kind of superficial. An NGO might look community-based, but if funding shapes priorities, it could end up listening upward to donors more than downward to residents.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong insight — 'listening upward vs. downward' is a meaningful distinction. Now flip it: given that tension, what conditions or circumstances do you think would allow an NGO to actually stay genuinely accountable to communities?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated the 'upward vs. downward accountability' concept quite well on their own. This is a key analytical insight for the paper. I should affirm it briefly and then push them toward the next part of the prompt — circumstances where NGOs are more effective — to keep moving forward rather than dwelling on this point.

exchanges.student: Maybe when the NGO works through long-term local relationships instead of short project cycles, and when community members actually have some decision-making power, not just get consulted. I also think local NGOs might do better than international ones here, although I’m not totally sure because local groups can have their own elite capture problems too.
exchanges.tutor: You're thinking carefully here — the elite capture caveat is an important one. Now, the prompt also asks about social movements. How do you see them as different from NGOs in the development context? What can they do that NGOs perhaps can't?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is developing nuanced thinking — they've identified favorable conditions (long-term relationships, genuine participation) and are already self-correcting on local vs. international NGOs by raising elite capture. This is sophisticated. I should affirm briefly and then redirect toward the social movements part of the prompt, which they haven't touched yet, to make sure the full reflection is covered.

exchanges.student: I think social movements can push for structural change more directly, like rights, land, labor, or state accountability, while NGOs often deliver services or work within existing policy limits. I’m a little stuck on how sharply to separate them, though, because some NGOs also do advocacy.
exchanges.tutor: That blurriness you're noticing is actually worth keeping in your paper — don't try to resolve it artificially. So here's a question: what does it tell us about civil society that the boundary between NGOs and social movements is fuzzy? Does that complicate or strengthen the case for civil society as a development actor?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the service-delivery vs. structural change distinction and is self-questioning about the boundary, which maps directly to the prompt's question about 'the boundary of civil society as an actor.' I should affirm the insight and push them to think about what that blurry boundary actually means for their argument rather than resolving it for them.

exchanges.student: I think it complicates it, because “civil society” starts sounding too neat when the actors have different goals, funding, and relationships to the state. But maybe it also strengthens the analysis if I say civil society isn’t one thing — some actors provide services, some mobilize pressure, and some do both.
exchanges.tutor: That's a genuinely strong analytical move. You now have the building blocks of a real argument. Can you try to write a few sentences — in your own words — that capture your overall position on NGOs and civil society as development actors? Something you could use as the core argument of your paper.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a sophisticated, nuanced position that addresses the prompt's question about civil society boundaries directly. This is essentially the core argument of a strong reflection paper. We've now covered all the main components of the prompt. I should briefly affirm and push them to start synthesizing — it's time to move from discussion to writing.

exchanges.student: Okay, maybe something like: NGOs can be effective development agents when they are embedded in local contexts, accountable to communities, and able to complement rather than replace the state. Their limits show up when donor priorities, short-term projects, or local power hierarchies distort participation. More broadly, civil society should not be treated as a single actor, because NGOs and social movements play different roles, especially with social movements being more important for challenging power and pushing structural change.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid core argument — you've covered effectiveness, limitations, and the NGO/social movement distinction well. One thing to push further: you say civil society plays different roles, but does context matter? Think about a fragile state, or a country with strong authoritarian control — would the boundary between these actors shift? Would their roles change?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid core argument that covers most of the prompt's components. It's well-structured and shows genuine thinking. However, they haven't addressed 'what is the boundary of civil society in particular development contexts' — the context-dependency piece is missing. I should give brief positive feedback and push them to think about that gap without giving it away.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think context matters a lot. In fragile or authoritarian settings, NGOs might fill service gaps because the state is weak or repressive, but that can also make them substitutes for the state in a way that’s not great long term. I’m guessing social movements may be more constrained there too, or forced into informal forms, but I’m not sure how to phrase that clearly.
exchanges.tutor: You don't need the perfect phrasing yet — just write it as you understand it and refine later. You now have all the key ideas you need. Try integrating everything into a full draft and come back with it — that's where the real work begins.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a strong contextual point and is now hesitating on phrasing — but per the rules, I should not help them with language or writing. I should redirect them to integrate this into their written attempt rather than workshopping phrasing with me. We've covered all the prompt's components across multiple exchanges, so it's time to push toward a full draft.

exchanges.student: Got it — I think I have the argument now, but I’m still a bit nervous about making it coherent in 700–800 words. Could you help me make a really simple outline first so I don’t end up repeating myself?
exchanges.tutor: That's your job! You already have the argument — try sketching the outline yourself first, then share it and we can discuss whether it holds together logically.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to help them build an outline, which would cross into doing the organizational work for them. Per the rules, I should not complete tasks for the student. Instead, I should redirect them to attempt the outline themselves and offer to give feedback on what they produce.

exchanges.student: Fair, okay. I’d try: intro with my main claim; paragraph on when NGOs are effective; paragraph on their limitations; paragraph on social movements and why civil society isn’t one unified actor; then a short part on how context like fragile/authoritarian states changes these roles; then conclusion. Does that seem too basic?
exchanges.tutor: That structure covers all the prompt's questions in a logical order — not too basic at all. Now write it. Bring back a draft and we'll work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a logical outline that maps well onto the prompt's components. It's not 'too basic' — it's actually well-aligned. I should affirm briefly and push them to start writing rather than over-planning. The concern about being 'too basic' doesn't need extended discussion.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. All key ideas — the donor accountability tension, elite capture, the NGO/social movement distinction, context-dependence — were drawn out of the student through targeted questions rather than stated by the tutor. The tutor consistently built on student responses, scaffolded progression from concept to argument to structure, and maintained concise, supportive, appropriately formative communication. No deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave the student a ready-to-submit answer or did the reasoning for them; every substantive insight (donor accountability, elite capture, civil society heterogeneity, context-dependence) was elicited from the student via Socratic questioning. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on correct student progress (e.g., affirming 'listening upward vs. downward,' the elite capture caveat, the blurriness between NGOs and movements) and diagnosed where the student was stuck before responding. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback, notably telling the student to keep the 'blurriness' rather than resolve it artificially, and redirecting outline work back to the student. 2.1–2.2: The conversation advanced steadily without repetition or off-topic drift, and the assignment prompt was kept central throughout. 3.1–3.2: Responses were concise and interactive, tone was warm and encouraging without being evaluative or grade-like.
